explicit_text: Carson Attractions began as a ticketing agency involved in selling tickets for events it promoted, eventually expanding to assist traveling entertainment groups and sporting events with advertising and promotion. My grandparents started presenting shows through Carson Attractions as early as 1916, beginning with opera singers like John McCormack. By the 1940s and beyond, the company was deeply involved in Tulsa’s entertainment scene, handling ticket sales for a variety of acts and managing the box office for the Tulsa Assembly Center. I grew up in the business, helping with promotions and eventually taking over after my father passed away.

This was one of the earliest experiences that led to the Carsons’ long involvement in presenting fine arts and entertainment events in the community.

explicit_text: Convention Hall, located at 105 West Brady in Tulsa, was the original venue where many of the events my family presented were held. It later became Tulsa Municipal Theater and then the Brady Theater, known as the Old Lady of Brady. I spent many days there in the 1940s as a child while my parents were involved in presenting shows, including the premiere of Oklahoma and performances by Gene Autry and Blackstone the Magician.

explicit_text: In the 1960s, my father was negotiating to present the Broadway show Damn Yankees in Tulsa. This story highlights how different the business was back then compared to today.

explicit_text: The Tulsa Convention Center, originally called the Tulsa Assembly Center, was completed around the time my father was asked to manage its box office. This was a major development because it required handling ticket sales for multiple large events, sometimes with over a hundred thousand tickets on sale at once. Carson Attractions took on this responsibility, expanding its role in the local entertainment scene.

explicit_text: Carol Channing came to Tulsa in 1966 to perform Hello, Dolly! at the Tulsa Municipal Theater, which was then in poor condition. Despite initial concerns from her crew about the theater’s safety and facilities, the show went on and broke attendance records. I developed a lasting relationship with Carol and her husband Charles Lowe because of that production.

It ended up breaking records for theatrical performances in Tulsa at that time.
